reasoning: CONFIRMED REAL but severity downgraded to low. The install_template() function at libs/functions.php:1317 is registered via admin_action_wpcaptcha_install_template (advanced-google-recaptcha.php:107) inside an is_admin() block. The function calls check_admin_referer('wpcaptcha_install_template') but has NO current_user_can() check — confirmed by grep across the entire function. The sibling install_wp301() on line 14 of functions.php DOES have current_user_can('administrator'), proving the developer knew how to add such checks. The sink (update_option(WPCAPTCHA_OPTIONS_KEY, $options)) is reachable with attacker-controlled $template key via sanitize_key($_GET['template']), which selects from a whitelist of built-in templates and merges them into plugin options. The claimed reachability ('any authenticated user with the nonce') is technically correct — the code imposes no capability gate. HOWEVER: the nonce wp_create_nonce('wpcaptcha_install_template') is ONLY generated in interface/tab_design.php:35, which is only rendered by main_page() which has current_user_can('manage_options') at admin.php:278. admin_action_ hooks require the user to be logged in (wp-admin enforces auth_redirect). Therefore practical exploitation requires admin-level access to obtain the nonce from the Design tab UI, making this effectively admin-only. The flaw is a missing capability check (broken access control) — the nonce is CSRF protection, not authorization — but the actual impact is limited: an attacker who already has the nonce (obtained from an admin) can overwrite design-only options (cosmetic settings). No data exfiltration, no code execution, no privilege escalation path. Real finding, but practical impact is low.
